onboarding.screen1_subtitle: Tell us a bit about your story — we'll turn it into the player's biography.
onboarding.screen2_subtitle: What defines you as an athlete and what drives you forward from here.

questions.spark: What was the moment you truly fell in love with football?
questions.obstacle: What was the biggest obstacle on the way?
questions.trait: What defines you on the pitch?
questions.dream: What's your biggest dream in football?

spark.tv_final: Watching a Cup / World Cup final on TV
spark.primeira_chuteira: Getting my first pair of boots
spark.volta_derrota: Coming back from my first hard defeat

obstacle.rejeicoes: I was rejected at many trials
obstacle.lesao: A serious injury nearly ended it all

dream.idolo_clube: Becoming an eternal legend at one club

varzea: {name} grew up kicking a ball barefoot on rough park pitches every Sunday, where the grass was more dirt than turf and every match ended in an argument.
{name}'s football began where it always begins — on uneven sandlot pitches, in raw kickabouts where the ball rolls over pebbles and tackles come in studs-up.
Before any academy, it was the sandlot that taught {name} the game — crooked pitch, harsh sun, and older boys who never went easy.
The sandlot was {name}'s first school: shots into goalposts made of flip-flops, pickup matches that lasted until dark, and the certainty that on that battered field, he was home.
It was on the dirt sandlots, with dust rising at every kick, that {name} discovered early he played a different game from the rest.
escolinha: {name} was spotted early at a neighborhood youth academy, the kind that charges symbolic fees and dreams big with every kid who turns up.
{name}'s football story began at a modest local academy, on a Wednesday training session that felt routine but ended up shaping a calling.
It was a neighborhood youth academy that opened the first doors for {name} — two sessions a week, a patient coach, and the first pair of boots that were truly his own.
Before the pressure of pro clubs, {name} learned the fundamentals at a simple academy — rondos, finishing drills, and the repeated lesson that talent without discipline takes nobody anywhere.
{name} got up early on Saturdays to train at a neighborhood youth academy, and it was there — among plastic cones and half-flat balls — that passion stopped being a game.
futsal: {name} learned the game on futsal courts, where there's no margin for error and the ball passes between your legs before you even understand what happened.
{name}'s technical edge comes from way back — years on indoor courts, closed gyms, tight dribbles, and quick low-corner finishes.
Before the grass, it was futsal that sculpted {name} — slick floors, walls as allies, and the obligation to think three passes ahead.
{name} started on futsal courts, the kind that fit at the back of any school, but where you learn the hardest art of all — playing with your head up.
The futsal school shaped everything in {name} — short touches, reading the game, and that good kind of restlessness from growing up playing 40 minutes without a breath.
rua: {name} started playing in his own street, dribbling between parked cars and celebrating goals before someone shouted 'car!'
{name}'s street became his first pitch — hot summer asphalt, scraped knees with every fall, and a gang of kids who played until their mothers called them in for dinner.
Before any club, before any academy, before anything — {name} learned the game in his own backyard, where the wall played centre-back and the gate was the goal.
{name} played so much football in his own street that the neighbors knew his name from the shouting — crossing, scoring, celebrating, restarting — always the same loop.
It was on that dead-end street, with the goal drawn in chalk on the wall, that {name} fell in love with football before he even knew what an official pitch looked like.
colegio: {name} broke through playing for his school team, those modest tournaments where standing out made you the talk of every classroom.
It was in school tournaments that {name} caught attention for the first time — school uniform, classmates as the crowd, and plays that travelled the whole hallway in the weeks that followed.
School was {name}'s first stage — recess turned into kickabouts, and finals between classrooms that he treated like cup deciders.
{name} started out playing for his school, in football improvised with backpacks for goalposts, but it taught him early how important playing under pressure is.
Before any formal invitation, {name} was already a name in school pickup matches, the kind where winning earned you bragging rights for the entire week.

pai: The first one to believe was his father, who'd leave work early on Wednesdays to drive him to training.
His father was his first coach, his first fan, and the first person to bet that the skinny kid had something different.
It was in his father's eyes, shining from the stands at every match, that he found strength on the days when everything seemed too hard.
His father didn't know much about tactics, but he knew his son — and he made sure every torn pair of sneakers got replaced, whatever it cost.
Father and son shared every win — from the first goal on a small pitch to the call that changed everything — always with the same line at the end of the day: 'tomorrow we train more'.
familia: His mother carried the job of believing before everyone else — patching up old kits, counting coins for tournament fees, holding things together on the days her son came home crying from training.
It was his grandmother who walked him down a club's first corridor, with the quiet determination of someone who knew something important was being born there.
An uncle, half pickup-game player, half improvised teacher, was the first to see it: 'this kid's got something different — keep pushing him'.
The whole family became a coaching staff — mother washing kits, sister filming matches on her phone, everyone saying the same thing: 'you can do this'.
At home, no one ever doubted — his mother held the faith when he doubted himself, and that made all the difference in the moments he almost gave up.
irmao: His older brother — that frustrated player who quit somewhere along the way — became his first teacher, with bad passes corrected in the backyard, drilled with 'again, this time right'.
It was by following his older brother everywhere that he learned the game — tagging along to the older boys' matches, getting bashed around in midfield, coming home bruised but stronger.
His brother was a hero at home long before any TV idol — every new move he learned came from him, and every compliment from him counted double.
When his older brother quit football to start working, the younger one quietly promised he'd play for both of them — and never went back on that.
It was his older brother who first said the line he'd remember forever — 'you play better than I did at your age — don't waste this'.
idolo: There was no flesh-and-blood mentor around — just a TV idol, plays rewatched on loop, and the kid's wish to do the same on the street pitch.
The TV idol did the work of inspiring — every goal celebrated at home, every dribble memorized, every roar copied on the small pitches the following week.
There was no one to teach at home, but there was the TV idol — a shirt number to imitate, a celebration to replicate, a distant model that somehow felt within reach.
He learned alone watching the greats, with sharp eyes and a brain wired for reading the game — taping the moves, repeating them on the small pitch, missing a lot, hitting some, adjusting again.
That idol from old YouTube videos became a long-distance teacher, and for every shot drilled at home there was a fragment of memorized play stored in his head.
sozinho: Without anyone to push him, he discovered football alone — tucking the ball under his arm and looking for whatever pickup match was running on the block.
It was sheer self-will that opened the road — no coach at home, no encouragement, just him, the ball, and the stubbornness not to quit.

He never had a real mentor — he discovered the game on his own skin, learned by failing, adjusted by watching, and built every skill like someone who learns to ride a bike with no one holding on behind.
He had only himself to push him — he was his own coach, his own critic, and his own fan in the empty stands of his imagination.
It was early in life that he understood no one was going to do it for him — he took his fate into his own hands and went hunting football wherever it could be found.

primeiro_gol: The day he understood he could live off this was in a kids' final, when he came on in the closing minutes and scored the winner — got carried home on shoulders and didn't sleep that night.

Everything changed on an afternoon he'd never forget — ball at his feet, his team losing, and the strike into the top corner that silenced the whole pitch — he walked off knowing this was what he'd do forever.
The turning point was a routine goal in some random pickup match — the ball went in, the team shouted, and something inside him caught fire and never went out.

The spark came from a goal no one expected — better opponent, game decided, and him hitting one from distance to watch the ball die in the back of the net while the world spun differently from there on.
It was scoring his first big goal, in a tournament few would remember later, that he understood — without needing words — what his profession would be for the rest of his life.
tv_final: It was watching a Cup final at home, with the family gathered and his heart in his mouth, that football stopped being a game and became destiny.
Football came into his life for good during a World Cup — watched in silence, the anthem sung standing up in the living room, and the childlike certainty that one day he'd be there too.
That final on TV became a watershed — he walked away from the screen with his head on fire, laced up his beat-up boots, and went to train alone in the backyard until it got dark.
All it took was a major final on television — the eruption of the crowd, the captain lifting the trophy — for him to know that's where he was heading, even if he didn't yet know the road.
It was in front of the TV, during a final the whole country stopped to watch, that something shifted — he left the couch in silence and never returned the boots to the box.
primeira_chuteira: The shift came on the birthday he got his first real pair of boots — those bright yellow plastic studs he didn't even want to take off to sleep.
The day he received his first own pair of boots — not borrowed, not handed down, not the wrong size — was the day he understood this was the real thing.
It wasn't a goal or a final — it was a box wrapped in cheap paper, with a pair of boots inside that took the family months of saving, that opened the door to everything.
The new boots stayed packed for a whole day before debuting, scared of getting them dirty — when he finally laced them up, he silently swore that gift wouldn't be wasted.
It was the first pair of boots that changed everything — not because of the model, not the brand, but the symbolic weight of knowing someone at home had believed enough to invest.
campeao_moleque: It was winning a neighborhood tournament, holding up a small trophy with dirty hands, that he felt for the first time what winning meant — and never wanted to lose that feeling.
The trophy was plastic, the tournament was amateur, but for him that title raised more than silverware — it raised an inner certainty that never came undone.
His first kid title, in a tournament no one outside his family would remember, became the most important thing in the world — and it was right there he decided to turn passion into a craft.
When his team won that modest tournament, and he was the name of the campaign, something inside him locked in for good — he discovered the podium was a place that fit him.
It was lifting a small trophy, on a sandlot, with a crowd made only of the players' parents, that he understood what being the protagonist meant — and never accepted any other role.
volta_derrota: The spark came inside out — in a heavy defeat that hurt deep, and in the conscious decision, still in the locker room, that the next one he wouldn't lose.
It was a defeat that captured football for him for real — he walked off the pitch crying, and showed up the next day for training before everyone else, with his heart on fire.
It wasn't winning that changed everything — it was a humiliating defeat, goals conceded in sequence, and the silence on the bus home that made him promise never to feel that again without reacting.
The hardest fall came early, and it was that fall that lifted him for real — every training session from then on carried the weight of the last defeat and the wish for it never to repeat.
The turning point was a humiliation on the pitch — lopsided scoreline, opposing fans laughing, and the silent decision that the kid who played for fun ended right there.

dinheiro: The road demanded sacrifice — old boots, bus rides when there was money, and months when it was choose between school supplies and the trial fee.
It wasn't easy — money was short for a lot, improvisation made up for almost everything, and the dream kept rolling inside torn sneakers.
Every cent counted — hand-me-down kits from an older brother, boots that pinched because there was no cash for new ones, and the silent shame he learned to turn into fuel.
Money was missing at almost every step of the way — but creativity made up for it, and it was in that scarcity that he toughened the parts of him that needed toughening.
Poverty was at the table, in the commute, in the boots — and it was in learning to turn matches around with little that he became good at turning matches around with little.
distancia: He lived far from any real club — two buses to get there, two to come back, and impossible hours that anyone with less hunger wouldn't have endured.
Distance was the invisible opponent — three hours commuting between home and training, every single day, and the constant temptation to just give up.
The bus became part of the routine before training even started — leaving before dawn, returning in the dark, and using the time in between to study or doze off between bumps.
Talent alone wasn't enough — to get to training, he had to leave home two hours early, cross the whole city, and still arrive with legs to run.
Anyone who lives far away knows it — every training session demanded logistics: an uncle's favor with a car, missing fare money, and the daily stubbornness not to quit out of exhaustion.
rejeicoes: He was rejected at trial after trial — big club, mid-sized club, small club — before someone finally stopped to look more patiently.
The doors closed several times before one opened — he walked away crying from more trials than he'd care to admit, but he always came back for the next one.
Every 'thanks, but no' became another brick in the wall he was building — there were so many 'no's that the 'yes' felt more like a dream than reality when it finally came.
He went through trials like someone collecting defeats — and it was exactly in that pile-up that he learned to step on the pitch with a knife between his teeth, with nothing to lose.
Rejection rode shotgun for years — and every time it seemed like he'd quit, something inside whispered one more, one more, one more.
lesao: A serious injury almost ended everything before it began — months without playing, expensive physio, and the silent fear of never being the same.
His knee gave out early, at a moment he thought he'd already made it — and the long road back from there was paved with patience, pain, and stubbornness.
The injury came out of nowhere and took everything with it — months immobilized, training watched from a distance, and the vertigo of seeing other players move forward while he stood still.
When the injury hit, it felt like the end — but it was during that recovery, away from the pitch, that he discovered he loved the game even more than he knew.
Coming back from a serious injury made him a different player — more aware of his body, more grateful for minutes on the pitch, more mentally ready to value every match.
familia_estudo: At home, they kept hammering on studies — 'football is a lottery, better get a degree' — and he learned early to balance schoolbooks and boots in the same day.
The family supported him, but worried — they wanted school, a stable profession, a real plan B — and he had to prove he could handle both at the same time.
There was pressure at home — finish school, keep grades up, don't bet only on your foot — and that pressure taught him to balance two lives in one routine.
Convincing the family was a battle of its own — every bad grade became a 'drop that ball and study for good' threat, and every win on the pitch became a counter-argument.
At home, the dream was viewed with suspicion — 'you can't count on that' — and he had to insist more than play, in the early days, just to keep being allowed to train.

raca: On the pitch, it's grit before anything else — tackling until the last minute, fighting for 50/50s no one else wants, chest puffed out even down by three.
Ask anyone who plays alongside him — the trademark is grit. The guy doesn't switch off — when the team sags, he's the one pulling them back up.
His thing is commitment — there's no match won in advance, no ball lost without a fight, no second half he doesn't run as hard as the first minute.
He always plays like it's the last game — runs for two, marks for three, drags the team along even on the days everything seems to be going wrong.
Anyone who faces him learns it fast — there's no magic formula here, just lungs, heart, and stubbornness from someone who refuses to lose without first leaving everything on the pitch.
frieza: On the pitch, it's coolness above all — ball on the line in the closing minutes, he treats it like training and converts as if he'd already seen the goal happen.
What separates him from others is the calm — the more the game tightens, the more he lowers his internal pace, and it's in that silence that he decides matches.
Under pressure, he becomes another person — steady pulse, clear decision, choice made before the opponent even understands what's about to happen.
Ask anyone who plays with him — the guy has no nerves. Decisive penalty, last-minute free kick, finishing one-on-one with the keeper — his head operates a level above.
The trademark is coolness in the closing minutes — when others tremble, he reads the match with clarity and resolves it with a simplicity that scares the opponent.
tecnica: On the pitch, technique comes above all — line-splitting passes, clean reception with his back to goal, finishes placed where the keeper can't reach.
His thing is elegance — ball glued to his foot, short dribbles that lift defenders off the ground, plays that stay in the crowd's head long after the final whistle.
Anyone watching catches it fast — every touch has intent, every pass has weight, every move was rehearsed a thousand times to look simple.
He plays like someone who understood beauty is an argument — flicked passes when he can, dribbles that humiliate when he must, finishes that decorate the frame of the goal.
The difference is in the detail — where others kick, he places; where others pass, he draws; where others run, he reads the game.
lideranca: He's the voice of command on the pitch — calls for responsibility, organizes the press, talks to the keeper, argues with the ref when he must.
A natural leader, with that kind of charisma that makes the team grow together — a gesture to demand more, a word to settle, an example to pull along.
In the locker room and on the pitch, he's a reference point — younger players look for him, veterans respect him, the coach counts on him for every important moment.
The shirt weighs differently when he wears it — not because of the number, but because of the role he takes on: organizing the team, motivating when they fall, restraining when they go too far.
Ask any teammate — the guy is a leader. Firm voice, right gesture, presence that organizes the chaos of the toughest matches.
irreverencia: On the pitch, it's pure swagger — a smile in the corner of his mouth during the rough match, nutmeg on the older centre-back, a celebration that never repeats.
He plays like someone who never forgot the pickup matches — cheeky dribble, a risky play when the game asks for balance, and that light touch that's contagious to the whole team.
His thing is daring — there's no easy play he won't complicate, no complicated play he won't try to simplify, and the crowd loves that unpredictability.

Anyone who faces him finds out the hard way — the guy doesn't respect the moment. Tied in a final? He pulls a flick. Playing for the trophy? He celebrates laughing. Plays as if the ball were an extension of the game.
The trademark is joy — he steps onto the pitch with the smile that's survived since the small fields, and that's exactly what makes him so hard to predict.

liga: Today, he aims at what he always dreamed of in a low voice — lifting the League with the club he supports, hearing the anthem and watching banners rise from the stands.
His dream is clear — winning the League wearing the shirt he'd worn since he was a kid, and giving the fans the title so many have waited too long for.
Deep down, it's always the same scene he projects in his head — trophy raised to the wind, the crowd in delirium, and that specific club's badge around his neck.
When asked what he wants from football, the answer comes ready — he wants to be League champion for the team he picked before he even knew how to kick straight.
More than any individual prize, what moves him is a single image — the League trophy rising in his hands, with the shirt he kissed so many times in childhood.
selecao: And the dream — that one he can describe in a single line: wear the national team shirt one day.
The target is clear — the national kit, the anthem at the mouth of the tunnel, the chance to represent his country with his chest out.
When asked what he still wants in football, the answer comes without hesitation — to hear the national anthem standing up, with the national jersey on, before a major decisive match.
The dream he keeps in silence is the national team — a call-up, even a single minute on the pitch, anything that proves it was all worth it.
He aims high — wants to hear the anthem with hand on heart, watch the flag rise, and know he represents millions when the ball rolls.
familia: His dream is small and giant at the same time — to support his family with football, give his mother the house she deserves, return in security what he received in faith.
At the end of the day, he plays for his own — so his mother doesn't have to work late anymore, so his brother can study wherever he wants, so his father can rest after a lifetime of struggle.
His engine has a name and an address — the home where he grew up, the people who paid for the boots, the hours no one saw but that he'd never forget.
When asked why he plays, the answer is familiar — to give back, to take care, to make sure every cent invested in him pays back as peace for those who invested.
His biggest dream fits in a small gesture — buying his mother a house, and watching her live there, knowing it was football that changed everything.
europa: His dream crosses the Atlantic — to play in Europe, hear the crowd in another language, wear a traditional club's shirt in a major league.
He aims at European football with the clarity of someone who grew up watching those finals on TV — wants to be there, wear that shirt, listen to that anthem before the match begins.
He knows where he wants to go — packed stadium in some European capital, immaculate pitch, broadcast to the whole world, and him on the field, part of it.
When he closes his eyes, the scenery is specific — Champions League knockouts, and him stepping onto the pitch for a major continental club, wearing the shirt on merit.
The destination he projects on the horizon is Europe — not as vanity, but as a yardstick — because that's where the best play, and he wants to be measured on that scale.
idolo_clube: His dream is to become a legend — not stat lines, not a quick stint, but kids' shirts decades later, a photo in the trophy room, a story told from generation to generation.
He wants to be one of those players whose name the crowd shouts even after he's no longer on the pitch — to become part of the club's history, not just a chapter.
He aims at longevity where so many aim at passing through — wants one club, one shirt, and the memory of fans turned into a monument over the years.
His goal is specific — to be the guy the fans remember forever, to earn a statue at the stadium entrance, to have a song sung decades after retirement.
He doesn't want to be good — he wants to be eternal. Statue, retired number, name on the stand. He wants to become a legend at the club he chooses to wear the shirt for.

closings: Now, at {club}, he writes the first page of a story that once seemed impossible to write.
And it's right at {club} that this whole journey finds its first professional chapter — the rest, from now on, is up to him.
Today, with the {club} shirt on his chest, he opens the first professional chapter of a journey that's barely begun.
The path arrives at {club}, and from here on every minute on the pitch counts as real history.
Everything that came before paved the ground — now, at {club}, what truly matters begins.
He puts on the {club} shirt for the first time knowing this is where his name starts being written for real.
The road to {club} was long; the road from {club} on, he promises, will be unforgettable.
He arrived at {club} with the humility of someone who knows where he came from, and the hunger of someone who knows where he wants to go.
At {club}, he finds the stage where everything he trained for can finally turn into results — and the time to prove it starts now.
His professional journey begins at {club}, with a backpack full of past and a head fully turned toward the next step.
It's at {club} that he debuts as a pro — and every training session, from here on, counts as a brick of the career he's about to build.
He steps onto the {club} pitch knowing the kid from the small fields and the pro stepping in now are the same person, only more whole.
Today he wears {club} for the first time — and what comes after this moment depends only on him and the ball.
{club} is the first professional club, but for him it's more than that — it's the chance that took its time to come, and one he doesn't intend to let slip.
He lands at {club} carrying everyone who believed in him — and ready to turn each of those bets into goals, matches, memories.
The era when he stops promising and starts delivering begins at {club} — everything else from here on will be an answer to what he proves now.
{club} is the first name on his professional road — and he silently guarantees it won't be the last important chapter of this story.
He steps into {club} convinced that every scar from the road here will turn into argument inside the four lines.
Now is the time — the official debut comes with the {club} shirt, and the kid from the small fields can finally be called a pro.
He puts on the {club} shirt with the familiar feeling of having lived this a thousand times in his imagination — only this time it's real.
At {club}, he opens the door that so often stayed shut at other trials — and he's ready to walk through running.
He steps onto the {club} pitch knowing this is where the promise phase ends and the answer phase begins.
His first paycheck will come from {club} — but for him, what really weighs is the chance to finally play for real.
The professional story begins now, at {club}, and he steps on the pitch like someone who knows exactly why he's there.
He debuts as a pro for {club}, with the calm certainty that every minute on the pitch is an answer to someone who doubted.
He arrives at {club} with no time to waste — not because time is short, but because every minute outside it was preparation in excess.
At {club}, he finally finds the place where his will can turn into an official record.
{club} is the stage of his pro debut — from here on, every match enters the stat line that will tell the rest of his life.
He steps into {club} knowing that everything he dreamed depends now only on him and what he shows between the four lines.
The stage where every training matters, every match counts, every minute becomes history — that begins at {club}.
The {club} shirt marks the start — and he knows this start needs to be a good one, because the next steps depend on this first.
At {club}, the first page is finally turned — and the whole book depends on what he writes from today.
{club} is just the beginning — and he steps on the pitch determined to make sure this beginning is unforgettable.
Today, at {club}, he steps onto a professional pitch for the first time — and carries with him everyone who bet this day would come.
Official debut at {club}, and the feeling is mixed — gratitude for having arrived, hunger to go far beyond.
He arrives at {club} without giving an interview — he prefers to talk with his foot, show with gestures, let every match speak for him.
At {club}, the kid from the small fields becomes a pro — and only now does the story begin to be written by the world's eyes.
The journey arrives at {club}, and from here on every play becomes material for the biography someone will one day want to tell.
{club} is the first level — and he didn't get this far to stop, he got here to climb higher.
He debuts at {club} with new boots, a ready mind, and the entire story of his past turning into fuel for the immediate future.
At {club}, finally, what was once a bedside dream becomes a contract on the desk — and the pro is born for real.
He arrives at {club} with the calm of someone who understood the road — and the urgency of someone who won't waste the time he earned.
He wears {club} aware that everything that came before was school; everything coming now is the test.
The first professional shirt is {club}'s, and he'll wear it like a tattoo — conviction on the chest, identity here to stay.
At {club}, the chance is finally in his hands — and he hasn't come this far to waste anything now.
He steps on the {club} pitch carrying a single certainty — the hard part is over, and the truly hard part is yet to come, but he's ready.
{club} is the debut, but he treats it as a prologue — because the real story, he swears, will be much bigger.
He arrives at {club} carrying the past as ID and the future as daily duty.
Today, with the {club} shirt, he steps into the pro world to write a story no one yet knows how to tell — only him.
At {club}, the chapter that matters begins — the rest, from here on, is up to him.
closings_free_agent: He now takes his first steps as a professional free agent, about to find the club that will mark his story.
The professional journey starts now, without a club yet — and every trial, every showcase, is a brick of the next chapter.
He debuts as a pro free agent, with the confidence of someone who knows the next step is just choosing where to land.
He steps onto the professional market knowing the road here was hard — and that the next club will reap the rewards.
He enters the professional career without a fixed club, but with the certainty that whoever bets on him next won't regret it.
The start is as a free agent — door open, future still blank, and the will intact to turn it all into history.
No club yet, but with the first page of the story ready to be written — all that's needed is the right invitation.
Now it's the pro part of the journey, and he treats the market like he treated everything before it — ready to prove his worth to whoever shows up.
He officially debuts in pro football as a free agent, and he knows it — it's only a matter of time before the next chapter begins.
His professional career begins wide open, no fixed shirt, but with the entire story of his past serving as fuel for whatever comes next.
